input_text:
SECTION_TITLE: Definition: Business Capability
CONTENT:
A business capability defines what an organization must be able to do to fulfill its mission and deliver value. It represents a stable, organization-wide function that is independent of specific processes, systems, or organizational structures. Enterprise architects use business capabilities to: • Capture cross-functional needs across business units. • Provide a common language for strategic planning and transformation.

schema_definition.independentOf: The subject entity does not depend on or is not affected by the object entity.
schema_definition.use: The subject entity employs or utilizes the object entity for a particular purpose.
schema_definition.captures: The subject entity identifies, encompasses, or takes into account the object entity.
schema_definition.provides: The subject entity supplies, offers, or makes available the object entity.

input_text:
SECTION_TITLE: Structure Summary
CONTENT:
Purpose and Benefits of Business Capabilities Role in Application Classification Business capabilities are used to determine whether a software-based system should be classified as a business application or merely a technical component. This classification is essential for maintaining a consistent and business-aligned IT landscape. Without a complete and validated capability map, it is difficult to analyze the IT landscape from a business perspective.

SECTION_TITLE: Differentiation from Business Processes
CONTENT:
Business capabilities and business processes serve distinct purposes: • Capabilities describe what the organization must be able to do. • Processes describe how those capabilities are executed. While overlaps may occur, the abstraction level and modeling intent are fundamentally different.

input_text:
SECTION_TITLE: Definition: Digital Solution
CONTENT:
In alignment with the Scaled Agile Framework (SAFe) and extended by Porsche-specific terminology, a Digital Solution is defined as follows: A Digital Solution is a cohesive construct that delivers business value by integrating multiple IT Products. These IT Products are modular, technically and logically bounded units that implement specific business functionalities. Together, they form the technical foundation of a Digital Solution. In addition to IT Products, a Digital Solution may also include: • Business processes • Methods and practices • Organizational structures

input_text:
SECTION_TITLE: Characteristics of a Digital Solution
CONTENT:
• Consists of IT Products Each IT Product is an application, business service, or business platform, including its associated technologies. These are delivered to internal users (employees) and/or external users (customers).
• Supports Business Capabilities IT Products enable business capabilities, processes, and data entities.
• Enables Operational Value Streams (OVS) Digital Solutions are designed to support and enhance OVS by delivering measurable business value.
• Is Developed Within a Single Agile Release Train (ART) Each Digital Solution is budgeted and managed within one portfolio and is developed by one or more agile teams within a single ART.
• Is Part of a Development Value Stream (DVS) The contributing teams are part of a DVS and are responsible for defining, building, validating, and releasing the IT Products that make up the Digital Solution.
• Supports Independent Delivery IT Products within a Digital Solution are potentially independently deployable and operable, ensuring resilience, scalability, and speed.

input_text:
SECTION_TITLE: Purpose and Benefits
CONTENT:
• A Digital Solution delivers end-to-end value to internal or external customers.
• Customers do not purchase isolated features or capabilities—they seek complete solutions that fulfill their desired outcomes.
• Digital Solutions enable organizations to align technical delivery with business strategy and customer needs.

schema_definition.doNotPurchase: The subject entity does not buy or acquire the object entity.
schema_definition.fulfill: The subject entity satisfies or meets the requirements or conditions specified by the object entity.
schema_definition.align: The subject entity brings into proper coordination or agreement with the object entity.
schema_definition.to: The subject entity is directed toward or intended for the object entity.
schema_definition.seek: The subject entity actively looks for or desires the object entity.

input_text:
SECTION_TITLE: Architectural Objects and Attributes
CONTENT:
Each object in LeanIX is represented by a dedicated Fact Sheet. These are interconnected through mappings to form a comprehensive, domain-oriented, and technical architecture. The attributes of each object are based on the LeanIX standard data model and are extended with Porsche-specific attributes, especially in the areas of: • Security • Data Protection • Governance Each Fact Sheet type includes mandatory fields required to meet PASS minimum standards. These fields are clearly marked.

input_text:
SECTION_TITLE: Definition: IT Component
CONTENT:
An IT Component is a foundational element within the technical architecture of an IT product. It operates independently of any business logic or context. IT components can either be: • Commercial off-the-shelf (COTS) products acquired from external vendors, or • Custom-developed software created in-house. They serve as the technical building blocks that enable the functionality and operation of IT products.

input_text:
SECTION_TITLE: Purpose and benefits
CONTENT:
IT components are essential to run, maintain, and change IT products. They may be: • Encapsulated within technical platforms, or • Directly integrated into the IT product. The use of IT components supports strategic architecture management by enabling: • Lifecycle planning of technical assets • Clear documentation of ownership, responsibilities, and competencies • Transparency in the technical landscape • Identification of redundancies (overengineering) or gaps (underengineering) in technical capabilities • Discovery of synergies that can inform IT planning and optimization Accurate and complete documentation of IT components also contributes to operational stability and reliability. Structure Summary Examples of IT Components To support a clear and practical understanding of the concept of IT components, this section provides categorized examples that reflect real-world technologies commonly used in enterprise IT landscapes.

input_text:
SECTION_TITLE: Purpose and benefits
CONTENT:
Structure Summary Examples of IT Components To support a clear and practical understanding of the concept of IT components, this section provides categorized examples that reflect real-world technologies commonly used in enterprise IT landscapes. These examples illustrate how IT components—whether commercial, open source, or developed in-house—serve as foundational building blocks within the technical architecture of IT products. Each example is mapped to a specific category, such as databases, middleware, operating systems, or security components, and highlights its role in enabling organizations to run, maintain, and change their IT solutions effectively. This structured overview helps stakeholders across architecture, business, and operations to better identify, classify, and manage IT components within tools like LeanIX.

input_text:
SECTION_TITLE: Definition: IT Product
CONTENT:
At Porsche, an IT Product is classified as either an Application or a Platform. Regardless of category, each IT Product must have clearly defined ownership and accountability. Applikationen An Application is an IT Product that delivers specific business functionality to support business processes—for example, by automating process steps. Applications contribute to the realization of business capabilities and are aligned with the value creation process.

SECTION_TITLE: Definition: IT Product
CONTENT:
Applications contribute to the realization of business capabilities and are aligned with the value creation process. Key characteristics: • Requires IT Components to operate • Includes tools, systems, software, and user-facing touchpoints • Provides functionality via graphical or text-based user interfaces • May optionally expose APIs • Always supports an operational value stream Platform A Platform is an IT Product that provides technical infrastructure, including IT Components and potentially other platforms. It serves as the foundation for developing, deploying, and operating Applications. Group Application Group Applications are Applications developed and operated by Group brands and used by Porsche.

input_text:
SECTION_TITLE: Purpose and Benefits
CONTENT:
From an enterprise architecture perspective, the IT Product is the central modeling object across all architecture layers. It enables: • Functional development planning • Portfolio and release planning • Centralized documentation of: o Security o Compliance o Data protection o Governance requirements The IT Product is the primary reference object for ensuring: • Compliance with the Federal Data Protection Act (BDSG) • Fulfillment of corporate due diligence and risk management obligations • Adherence to ISO 27001 – Information Security • Implementation of BSI IT-Grundschutz (German Federal Office for Information Security) Additionally, IT Products serve as information hubs for integration with enterprise tools and processes, such as: • OneTrust (data processing records) • CMDB (change and asset management) For further details, refer to the Systems Integration chapter.

input_text:
SECTION_TITLE: Lifecycle Management in LeanIX Fact Sheets
CONTENT:
Each Fact Sheet type in LeanIX can be assigned a lifecycle. Whether this assignment is mandatory depends on the specific Fact Sheet type and is indicated on its detail page. Recommended Practice Regardless of whether the lifecycle is mandatory, it is strongly recommended to provide the following information: • Start of productive use (Go-Live date) • Decommissioning date (if known) Depending on the desired level of detail, additional lifecycle milestones can also be modeled (e.g., development start, pilot phase, sunset planning). Benefits Accurate lifecycle data supports: • Strategic planning and portfolio management • Compliance with regulatory and audit requirements • Impact analysis for changes and decommissioning • Architecture governance across all layers

input_text:
SECTION_TITLE: Organizational Responsibilities
CONTENT:
Each Fact Sheet in LeanIX must include an assignment of organizational responsibility. This responsibility can be defined at two levels: • Organizational Structure Level (e.g., departments, business units) • Process Organization Level (e.g., Agile Release Trains, portfolios) For certain Fact Sheet types, a distinction may also be made between functional (business) responsibility and technical (IT) responsibility. At least one form of responsibility must be specified—either at the organizational structure level or the process organization level. Ideally, both levels should be documented to ensure full traceability and accountability.

schema_definition.mayDistinguishBetween: The subject entity can make a differentiation or distinction between the types or categories specified by the object entity.
schema_definition.mustInclude: The subject entity is required to contain or have the element or component specified by the object entity.
schema_definition.examples: The object entities are instances or specific cases that illustrate or belong to the category represented by the subject entity.
schema_definition.both_levels: The subject entity refers to the two specified levels or categories considered together as a combined concept or requirement.

input_text:
SECTION_TITLE: System Integration: LeanIX ↔ BIC
CONTENT:
To support enterprise architecture modeling, especially the business architecture layer, LeanIX relies on the availability of active business processes. For this reason, a synchronization interface is in place: Process Import from BIC to LeanIX • All active business processes modeled in BIC are automatically imported into LeanIX. • These processes are available in LeanIX for reference and linkage to other architectural elements (e.g., applications, capabilities, initiatives). • If an application has been assigned to a process step in BIC, this assignment is also synchronized to LeanIX Application Export from LeanIX to BIC • All active IT products in LeanIX are synchronized to BIC as applications.

input_text:
SECTION_TITLE: Key Characteristics of an IT Component
CONTENT:
• It is a hardware or software unit with a clearly defined and dedicated use.
• It can be installed, enabled, disabled, or removed from an operational perspective.
• It represents the smallest modelable unit that forms part of an information system.
• It can be independently modified, distributed, or replaced by third parties.
• It has a managed vendor or manufacturer support lifecycle.
• It has a known vendor, manufacturer, or developer.

SECTION_TITLE: Checklist for Classifying Architecture Objects
CONTENT:
[
{"question": "1. Does the object provide purely technical functionality without business logic?", "if_yes_": "→ IT Component", "if_no_": "→ Proceed to Question 2"},
{"question": "2. Does the object directly support a business process?", "if_yes_": "→ IT Product", "if_no_": "→ Proceed to Question 3"},
{"question": "3. Is the object organizational, physical, or contractual in nature?", "if_yes_": "→ Not an IT Component", "if_no_": "→ Proceed to Question 4"},
{"question": "4. Is the object actively used by end users?", "if_yes_": "→ Likely an IT Product", "if_no_": "→ Possibly technical infrastructure"},
{"question": "5. Does the object consist of multiple technical components?", "if_yes_": "→ IT Product or IT Service", "if_no_": "→ Evaluate as a single component"},
{"question": "6. Can the object be documented in architecture tools like LeanIX?", "if_yes_": "→ Check if it fits as Product or Component", "if_no_": "→ Likely not directly modelable"}
]

input_text:
SECTION_TITLE: Checklist for Classifying Architecture Objects
CONTENT:

SECTION_TITLE: General Modeling Rules
CONTENT:
Subscriptions In LeanIX, subscriptions are used to assign individuals and roles to a Fact Sheet. There are two main types of subscriptions: • Responsible: Individuals accountable for the content and maintenance of the Fact Sheet. • Observer: Individuals who are informed but not responsible for the content. The specific roles that can be assigned to a Fact Sheet are defined in the modeling guidelines for each Fact Sheet type.

SECTION_TITLE: Lifecycle Management in LeanIX Fact Sheets
CONTENT:
Each Fact Sheet type in LeanIX can be assigned a lifecycle. Whether this assignment is mandatory depends on the specific Fact Sheet type and is indicated on its detail page. input_text:
SECTION_TITLE: Organizational Responsibilities
CONTENT:
Each Fact Sheet in LeanIX must include an assignment of organizational responsibility. This responsibility can be defined at two levels: • Organizational Structure Level (e.g., departments, business units) • Process Organization Level (e.g., Agile Release Trains, portfolios) For certain Fact Sheet types, a distinction may also be made between functional (business) responsibility and technical (IT) responsibility. At least one form of responsibility must be specified—either at the organizational structure level or the process organization level. Ideally, both levels should be documented to ensure full traceability and accountability.

CONTENT:
Predecessor Relations In LeanIX, the Successor and Predecessor relations are used to model the version history of Fact Sheets over time. These relations should be applied whenever a new version of an existing Fact Sheet is created to replace an older version. Successor relations can also be used while the previous version is still active and in productive use.

input_text:
SECTION_TITLE: Responsibility for IT Components
CONTENT:
The definition of who is responsible for an IT component continues to lead to heated discussions in the company. Especially due to the establishment of the so-called departmental IT (P51), we are in a dilemma here from an IT point of view. The person specified on a component is typically the person responsible for that component for the entire organization in terms of operations, maintenance, security, and compliance. This regulation is also not a problem for centrally provided components, but it causes problems in many other contexts and poses challenges for IT.

input_text:
SECTION_TITLE: Characteristics of an IT Product
CONTENT:
• It bundles business functions in a logically and functionally bounded way to process data.
• It may include custom-developed elements such as application code, configuration data, macros, etc.
• It can be a third-party software product, used as SaaS, cloud-based, or on-premises.
• It may represent a bundle of technical components that together provide infrastructure or business platforms.

SECTION_TITLE: Modeling Rule: When to Create a Separate IT Product
CONTENT:
Even if the same software is used across departments (e.g., CATIA, MATLAB), a separate IT Product must be modeled if: • It is used in a different business or logical context • It processes different types of data, possibly with different classifications • It involves different organizational responsibilities • It runs on different infrastructures (e.g., cloud vs. on-prem, Windows vs. Linux)

SECTION_TITLE: General Modeling Rules
CONTENT:
Subscriptions In LeanIX, subscriptions are used to assign individuals and roles to a Fact Sheet. There are two main types of subscriptions: • Responsible: Individuals accountable for the content and maintenance of the Fact Sheet. • Observer: Individuals who are informed but not responsible for the content. The specific roles that can be assigned to a Fact Sheet are defined in the modeling guidelines for each Fact Sheet type.

input_text:
SECTION_TITLE: Lifecycle Management in LeanIX Fact Sheets
CONTENT:
Each Fact Sheet type in LeanIX can be assigned a lifecycle. Whether this assignment is mandatory depends on the specific Fact Sheet type and is indicated on its detail page. Recommended Practice Regardless of whether the lifecycle is mandatory, it is strongly recommended to provide the following information: • Start of productive use (Go-Live date) • Decommissioning date (if known) Depending on the desired level of detail, additional lifecycle milestones can also be modeled (e.g., development start, pilot phase, sunset planning). Benefits Accurate lifecycle data supports: • Strategic planning and portfolio management • Compliance with regulatory and audit requirements • Impact analysis for changes and decommissioning • Architecture governance across all layers

input_text:
SECTION_TITLE: Organizational Responsibilities
CONTENT:
Each Fact Sheet in LeanIX must include an assignment of organizational responsibility. This responsibility can be defined at two levels: • Organizational Structure Level (e.g., departments, business units) • Process Organization Level (e.g., Agile Release Trains, portfolios) For certain Fact Sheet types, a distinction may also be made between functional (business) responsibility and technical (IT) responsibility. At least one form of responsibility must be specified—either at the organizational structure level or the process organization level. Ideally, both levels should be documented to ensure full traceability and accountability.

Many IT products and applications at Porsche are developed and operated by Agile Release Trains (ARTs). Others are managed within specific portfolios. SECTION_TITLE: Modeling Version History with Successor and Predecessor
CONTENT:
Relations In LeanIX, the Successor and Predecessor relations are used to model the version history of Fact Sheets over time. These relations should be applied whenever a new version of an existing Fact Sheet is created to replace an older version. Successor relations can also be used while the previous version is still active and in productive use.

input_text:
SECTION_TITLE: General Modeling Rules
CONTENT:
Lifecycle Management in LeanIX Fact Sheets Each Fact Sheet type in LeanIX can be assigned a lifecycle. Whether this assignment is mandatory depends on the specific Fact Sheet type and is indicated on its detail page. Recommended Practice Regardless of whether the lifecycle is mandatory, it is strongly recommended to provide the following information: • Start of productive use (Go-Live date) • Decommissioning date (if known) Depending on the desired level of detail, additional lifecycle milestones can also be modeled (e.g., development start, pilot phase, sunset planning). Benefits Accurate lifecycle data supports: • Strategic planning and portfolio management • Compliance with regulatory and audit requirements • Impact analysis for changes and decommissioning • Architecture governance across all layers

SECTION_TITLE: Interfaces to External Systems
CONTENT:
In enterprise architecture, it is common for IT Products to interact with external systems— for example, to exchange data with banks, government agencies, or third-party services. These systems are considered external if they: • Are not part of the Porsche network (including ToGe networks), • Are not operated by PAG, a subsidiary, or a group brand, • Are not part of the internal Porsche IT landscape. Important: External systems must not be modeled as IT Products in LeanIX. Instead, interactions with external systems are documented exclusively through Interface Fact Sheets.

input_text:
SECTION_TITLE: Technical Implementation of Interfaces
CONTENT:
Interfaces are not always implemented directly by the IT Product that provides or consumes them. In many cases, middleware platforms, API gateways, or integration services handle the technical implementation. This separation between logical ownership and technical realization is essential for accurate modeling and architecture governance. Modeling Guidelines: • The logical interface is modeled between the provider and consumer IT Products.

[
{"section": "Introduction", "key_concept": "Interface Fact Sheet", "description__rule": "Object in LeanIX to model data exchange between IT Products", "examples": "—"},
{"section": "Conceptual Overview", "key_concept": "Business-oriented modeling", "description__rule": "Focus on data flow, transfer type, frequency, and IT components", "examples": "—"},
{"section": "Interface Creation", "key_concept": "Consumer modeling allowed", "description__rule": "Consumers may create interfaces if not modeled by providers", "examples": "User creates interface for consumed API"},
{"section": "Naming Convention", "key_concept": "Source + Technology + Target (optional)", "description__rule": "Improves discoverability and transparency", "examples": "LeanIX EAM GraphQL, LeanIX EAM Collibra"},
{"section": "Responsibility Assignment", "key_concept": "Responsible = IT Product owner", "description__rule": "Auto-assigned if missing", "examples": "Subscription inherited from IT Product"},
{"section": "Orphan Interfaces", "key_concept": "Tag = \"to delete\"", "description__rule": "Archived after 30 days if unresolved", "examples": "Interface without IT Product link"},
{"section": "Versioning", "key_concept": "New version = type/content change", "description__rule": "Optional, based on usage", "examples": "v1 used by Product A, v2 by Product B"},
{"section": "External Systems", "key_concept": "External systems not modeled as IT Products", "description__rule": "Use Interface Fact Sheet only", "examples": "External: Zollbehörde REST, External: SCHUFA SOAP"},
{"section": "External System Definition", "key_concept": "Outside Porsche network", "description__rule": "Not operated by PAG, subsidiaries, or group brands", "examples": "Banks, SCHUFA, Customs, Tax Authorities"},
{"section": "External Interface Modeling", "key_concept": "Consumer-side responsibility", "description__rule": "Interface created and owned by consuming IT Product", "examples": "Zollabfertigung consumes Customs API"},
{"section": "Technical Implementation", "key_concept": "Middleware or platform IT Product", "description__rule": "Technical delivery handled by separate IT Product", "examples": "Porsche Developer Hub implements LeanIX EAM GraphQL"},
{"section": "Technical vs Logical Layer", "key_concept": "Separation of concerns", "description__rule": "Logical interface between systems, technical via platform", "examples": "CRM to ERP Sync via Integration Hub"},
{"section": "Feature Status", "key_concept": "Technical architecture section (WIP)", "description__rule": "Not yet available in LeanIX as of July 2025", "examples": "—"},
{"section": "Organizational Modeling", "key_concept": "No direct org assignment on interface", "description__rule": "Responsibilities are derived from the assigned provider IT Product", "examples": "Interface inherits responsibility from LeanIX as provider"}
]

SECTION_TITLE: Modeling Implications in LeanIX
CONTENT:
• Interfaces are central integration points that connect strategic initiatives and operational systems.
• Initiatives may depend on the availability or design of specific interfaces to achieve business goals.
• IT Products consume or provide interfaces, which must be modeled to reflect technical dependencies.
• The diagram supports cross-layer traceability from strategy to execution in LeanIX.

SECTION_TITLE: Introduction
CONTENT:
• Structural organization data is sourced from the Volkswagen Corporate Directory (VCD). This directory centrally provides data for Porsche AG (PAG) and its subsidiaries. The data is synchronized daily into LeanIX.

input_text:
SECTION_TITLE: Modeling Consumers vs. Responsibilities
CONTENT:
• If an IT Product is used by all departments within a main division (e.g., "FD"), it is sufficient to assign "FD" as the consuming unit.
• There is no need to list each sub-department individually.
• This is necessary to identify the appropriate manager when no individual contact is assigned to a Fact Sheet.
• Example: If the responsible unit is "FDB1", then "FDB1" must be assigned.

input_text:
SECTION_TITLE: Automatic Updates and Lifecycle Management
CONTENT:
One key benefit of centralized organization management is the automatic update of organizational structures during reorganizations (e.g., PPO changes). • If an organizational unit is dissolved or merged, LeanIX checks whether it is still linked to any objects. o If no links exist, the Fact Sheet is archived and becomes unusable. o If links exist, the Fact Sheet is tagged as "Legacy" ( ) Important: Fact Sheets tagged with "Legacy" ( ) must not be used in modeling.

input_text:
SECTION_TITLE: Introduction
CONTENT:
Whether this assignment is mandatory depends on the specific Fact Sheet type and is indicated on its detail page. Recommended Practice Regardless of whether the lifecycle is mandatory, it is strongly recommended to provide the following information: • Start of productive use (Go-Live date) • Decommissioning date (if known) Depending on the desired level of detail, additional lifecycle milestones can also be modeled (e.g., development start, pilot phase, sunset planning). Benefits Accurate lifecycle data supports: • Strategic planning and portfolio management • Compliance with regulatory and audit requirements • Impact analysis for changes and decommissioning • Architecture governance across all layers

input_text:
SECTION_TITLE: Best Practices
CONTENT:
• Use Parent/Child relations to group related Fact Sheets under a common context.
• Ensure that the hierarchy reflects actual ownership or architectural structure.
• Avoid circular or ambiguous relationships.
• Document the rationale for the hierarchy in the description field of the Fact Sheet if needed.
• If the Parent/Child relation is not applicable, explicitly set the field to “Leave empty” to ensure clarity and data quality.

SECTION_TITLE: Introduction
CONTENT:
This page describes the modeling guidelines for Digital Solutions in the context of enterprise architecture at Porsche. It is based on the general modeling guidelines of PAG and includes specific instructions for creating and maintaining Digital Solution fact sheets in LeanIX. The creation and editing of a Digital Solution fact sheet follows the standard procedure for creating a general fact sheet as described in "How to create a fact sheet". General Modeling Rules Lifecycle Management in LeanIX Fact Sheets Each Fact Sheet type in LeanIX can be assigned a lifecycle.

input_text:
SECTION_TITLE: Introduction
CONTENT:
General Modeling Rules Lifecycle Management in LeanIX Fact Sheets Each Fact Sheet type in LeanIX can be assigned a lifecycle. Whether this assignment is mandatory depends on the specific Fact Sheet type and is indicated on its detail page. Recommended Practice Regardless of whether the lifecycle is mandatory, it is strongly recommended to provide the following information: • Start of productive use (Go-Live date) • Decommissioning date (if known) Depending on the desired level of detail, additional lifecycle milestones can also be modeled (e.g., development start, pilot phase, sunset planning). Benefits Accurate lifecycle data supports: • Strategic planning and portfolio management • Compliance with regulatory and audit requirements • Impact analysis for changes and decommissioning • Architecture governance across all layers

input_text:
SECTION_TITLE: Organizational Responsibilities
CONTENT:
Each Fact Sheet in LeanIX must include an assignment of organizational responsibility. This responsibility can be defined at two levels: • Organizational Structure Level (e.g., departments, business units) • Process Organization Level (e.g., Agile Release Trains, portfolios) For certain Fact Sheet types, a distinction may also be made between functional (business) responsibility and technical (IT) responsibility. At least one form of responsibility must be specified—either at the organizational structure level or the process organization level. Ideally, both levels should be documented to ensure full traceability and accountability.

CONTENT:

Each Digital Solution must include at least one assigned IT Product. These IT Products represent the technical building blocks that deliver business functionality and are developed and maintained by agile teams within the ART.

input_text:
SECTION_TITLE: Introduction
CONTENT:
This page describes the modeling guidelines for initiatives in the context of enterprise architecture at Porsche. It is based on the general modeling guidelines of PAG and includes specific instructions for creating and maintaining initiative fact sheets in LeanIX. The creation and editing of an initiative fact sheet follows the standard procedure for creating a general fact sheet as described in "How to create a fact sheet".

input_text:
SECTION_TITLE: General Modeling Rules
CONTENT:
Subscriptions In LeanIX, subscriptions are used to assign individuals and roles to a Fact Sheet. There are two main types of subscriptions: • Responsible: Individuals accountable for the content and maintenance of the Fact Sheet. • Observer: Individuals who are informed but not responsible for the content. The specific roles that can be assigned to a Fact Sheet are defined in the modeling guidelines for each Fact Sheet type.

input_text:
SECTION_TITLE: Lifecycle Management in LeanIX Fact Sheets
CONTENT:
Each Fact Sheet type in LeanIX can be assigned a lifecycle. Whether this assignment is mandatory depends on the specific Fact Sheet type and is indicated on its detail page. Recommended Practice Regardless of whether the lifecycle is mandatory, it is strongly recommended to provide the following information: • Start of productive use (Go-Live date) • Decommissioning date (if known) Depending on the desired level of detail, additional lifecycle milestones can also be modeled (e.g., development start, pilot phase, sunset planning). Benefits Accurate lifecycle data supports: • Strategic planning and portfolio management • Compliance with regulatory and audit requirements • Impact analysis for changes and decommissioning • Architecture governance across all layers

input_text:
SECTION_TITLE: Organizational Responsibilities
CONTENT:
Each Fact Sheet in LeanIX must include an assignment of organizational responsibility. This responsibility can be defined at two levels: • Organizational Structure Level (e.g., departments, business units) • Process Organization Level (e.g., Agile Release Trains, portfolios) For certain Fact Sheet types, a distinction may also be made between functional (business) responsibility and technical (IT) responsibility. At least one form of responsibility must be specified—either at the organizational structure level or the process organization level. Ideally, both levels should be documented to ensure full traceability and accountability.

SECTION_TITLE: General Modeling Rules
CONTENT:
Subscriptions In LeanIX, subscriptions are used to assign individuals and roles to a Fact Sheet. There are two main types of subscriptions: • Responsible: Individuals accountable for the content and maintenance of the Fact Sheet. • Observer: Individuals who are informed but not responsible for the content. The specific roles that can be assigned to a Fact Sheet are defined in the modeling guidelines for each Fact Sheet type.

SECTION_TITLE: Lifecycle Management in LeanIX Fact Sheets
CONTENT:
Each Fact Sheet type in LeanIX can be assigned a lifecycle. Whether this assignment is mandatory depends on the specific Fact Sheet type and is indicated on its detail page. Recommended Practice Regardless of whether the lifecycle is mandatory, it is strongly recommended to provide the following information: • Start of productive use (Go-Live date) • Decommissioning date (if known) Depending on the desired level of detail, additional lifecycle milestones can also be modeled (e.g., development start, pilot phase, sunset planning). Benefits Accurate lifecycle data supports: • Strategic planning and portfolio management • Compliance with regulatory and audit requirements • Impact analysis for changes and decommissioning • Architecture governance across all layers

input_text:
SECTION_TITLE: Organizational Responsibilities
CONTENT:
Each Fact Sheet in LeanIX must include an assignment of organizational responsibility. This responsibility can be defined at two levels: • Organizational Structure Level (e.g., departments, business units) • Process Organization Level (e.g., Agile Release Trains, portfolios) For certain Fact Sheet types, a distinction may also be made between functional (business) responsibility and technical (IT) responsibility. At least one form of responsibility must be specified—either at the organizational structure level or the process organization level. Ideally, both levels should be documented to ensure full traceability and accountability.

CONTENT:

input_text:
SECTION_TITLE: Definition: Organization
CONTENT:
Organizations represent the users/owners of Applications or other business objetcts that can be modeled within different dimensions to create hierarchical structures. In Porsche AG's LeanIX, we distinguish between the elements of the structural organization and the elements of the process organization according to SAFe. The elements of the organizational structure represent the organizational structure of Porsche AG and its subsidiaries. The process organization is defined by the SAFe organization and is divided into portfolios and ARTs.

SECTION_TITLE: Definition: Tech Capability
CONTENT:

It describes the core function or purpose of a technology, such as a database, operating system, or server software. Unlike rapidly evolving technologies, the technical capability model remains relatively stable over time and is industry-agnostic.
